statement: Agriculture is one of the largest sources of greenhouse gas emissions in Denmark, accounting for approximately 11–12 million tonnes of CO2-equivalent annually, or roughly one-third of Denmark's total domestic emissions (which were approximately 33–35 million tonnes CO2-eq in 2023). This makes Danish agriculture proportionally one of the most emission-intensive agricultural sectors in the EU. The primary sources are: methane from livestock (particularly cattle), nitrous oxide from fertiliser application and manure management, and CO2 from drained peatlands/wetlands (formerly the largest single agricultural emission source). Denmark's intensive agricultural sector — which produces far more food than the country consumes (Denmark is a major exporter of pork, dairy, and grain) — has faced increasing pressure under the EU's climate framework to reduce emissions. In 2024, Denmark became the first country in the world to impose a direct tax on agricultural greenhouse gas emissions (effective 2030), with a rate of DKK 300 per tonne of CO2-equivalent rising to DKK 750 by 2035.
source_name: Danish Environmental Protection Agency (Miljøstyrelsen); Statistics Denmark; Danish Climate Council (Klimarådet)
caveats: Agricultural emission accounting is methodologically complex. The one-third share refers to Denmark's national inventory and includes land-use related emissions from agriculture. Different accounting boundaries (e.g., including/excluding CO2 from land use change and forestry) produce different percentages. The carbon tax legislation passed in 2024 is scheduled to take effect in 2030, and the final rates may be adjusted.

related_entries.statement: Denmark's Green Tripartite Agreement (Grøn trepart), concluded in 2024 between the government, agricultural organisations, and other stakeholders, includes ambitious land-use conversion targets. The agreement targets the conversion of approximately 390,000 hectares of agricultural land: approximately 250,000 hectares to forest and approximately 140,000 hectares to rewetted peatlands/wetlands. This represents approximately 15% of Denmark's cultivated agricultural area (approximately 2.6 million hectares total). The rewetting of drained peatlands is expected to be the single most cost-effective agricultural emission reduction measure, as drained organic soils emit significant quantities of CO2. The conversion programme is partly funded by the agricultural emissions tax and partly by direct government expenditure. Farmers whose land is converted will receive compensation. The agreement is part of Denmark's broader strategy to meet its legally binding target of 70% greenhouse gas emission reduction by 2030 (relative to 1990 levels) under the Danish Climate Act (Klimaloven, 2020).

Denmark has achieved substantial greenhouse gas emission reductions since 1990. According to the Danish Energy Agency and the European Environment Agency, Denmark's total greenhouse gas emissions declined from approximately 70 million tonnes CO2-equivalent in 1990 to approximately 33–35 million tonnes in 2023, a reduction of roughly 50%. The steepest declines came from the energy sector (transition from coal to wind power: Denmark now generates over 50% of electricity from wind) and the waste sector. However, agricultural emissions have been more resistant to reduction, declining only approximately 10–15% over the same period. Denmark's Climate Act (Klimaloven, 2020) established a legally binding target of 70% emission reduction by 2030 relative to 1990 and climate neutrality by 2050 — among the most ambitious in the EU. Denmark has been frequently cited in Icelandic EU debates as an example of how EU membership and climate policy interact.

citations.report_title: 390.000 hektarar af landbúnaðarlandi breytast í skóg og votlendi

claims.claim_text: Landbúnaður er einn stærsti losunarþáttur Danmerkur og losar 11-12 milljónir tonna CO2-ígilda.
Landbúnaður stendur fyrir um þriðjung af heildarlosun gróðurhúsalofttegunda í Danmörku.
Landbúnaður og matvælaiðnaður skapa 20-25% af heildarútflutningi Danmerkur.
